name: Vyasatirtha
dates_notes: Vijayanagara court records (Kṛṣṇadevarāya's preceptor); dates very well-established.

dates_evidence.description: Vijayanagara royal records, especially Kṛṣṇadevarāya's preceptor-status documents.
Manuscript colophons across the Nyāyāmṛta and Tarka-Tāṇḍava.

lineage_polemical.note: Vyāsatīrtha's Nyāyāmṛta is the structural target of Madhusūdana's Advaita-Siddhi.

core_thesis: Vyāsatīrtha is the architect of *Navya-Dvaita*: Madhva's *Tattvavāda* re-stated in the technical idiom of *Navya-Nyāya* and pushed to its dialectical limit. His positive metaphysics is straightforwardly Mādhva — *svatantra-paratantra* hierarchy, *pañca-bheda*, *viśeṣa*, *bimba-pratibimba*, *sākṣi*-pramāṇic, with Viṣṇu as the only *svatantra-tattva*. His distinctive contribution is methodological: he engineers Mādhva positions as inferences in *Navya-Nyāya* form, with explicit *vyāpti*-statements, *upādhi*-tests, and *avacchedaka*-conditions, so that the school can engage Gaṅgeśa's tradition on equal technical footing. The *Nyāyāmṛta* is the resulting masterwork — a systematic dismantling of the Advaitic *mithyātva*-thesis under each of its five canonical definitions, accompanied by positive *anumāna*s for *bheda*. The *Tarka-Tāṇḍava* turns the same machinery against the Naiyāyikas. Together these texts elevate Dvaita from a regional commentarial tradition to a pan-Indian dialectical force; Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* is precisely the response *Nyāyāmṛta* compelled, and the *Nyāyāmṛta–Advaita-Siddhi–Tarangiṇī–Laghu-Candrikā* cycle is the high-water polemic of late Vedānta.

engaged_works.title: Nyayamrta
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the most sophisticated Dvaita critique of Advaitic mithyātva.
engaged_works.summary: The *Nyāyāmṛta* ("Nectar of Reasoning") is the definitive Dvaita anti-Advaita polemic and the most technically sophisticated text the school produced. Structured in four *paricchedas*, it dismantles Advaitic *mithyātva* under each of its five canonical definitions — *sad-asad-vilakṣaṇatva*, *jñāna-nivartyatva*, *sat-pratiyogika-bheda*, *abhāva-vilakṣaṇatva*, *svāśraya-niṣṭha-atyantābhāva-pratiyogitva* — showing for each that the proposed *lakṣaṇa* either over-extends to *Brahman*, fails to apply to *prapañca*, or smuggles in a contradiction. Vyāsatīrtha then mounts positive *anumāna*s for *bheda* and against the Advaitic readings of *tat tvam asi*, *aham brahmāsmi*, and the *neti neti* passages, with full *Navya-Nyāya* apparatus (*vyāpti*, *upādhi*, *avacchedaka*, *paryāpti*). The work compelled Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* as its structural reply; the resulting cycle (*Nyāyāmṛta* → *Advaita-Siddhi* → *Tarangiṇī* → *Laghu-Candrikā* → *Nyāyāmṛta-Kaṇṭakoddhāra*) is the longest sustained inter-school dialectical engagement in classical Indian philosophy. The text remains the obligatory base-text for any serious Dvaita training in *khaṇḍana*-dialectic.

engaged_works.title: Tarka-Tandava
engaged_works.summary: The *Tarka-Tāṇḍava* ("Dance of Reasoning") is Vyāsatīrtha's frontal engagement with *Navya-Nyāya* — most directly with the school of Gaṅgeśa and the *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi* tradition. The work is structured as a *prakaraṇa*-by-*prakaraṇa* critique of Naiyāyika positions on *pramāṇa-lakṣaṇa*, *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna* (especially *vyāpti-grahaṇa*), *upamāna*, and *śabda*, conducted in the same Navya-Nyāya idiom the opponents use. Vyāsatīrtha's most influential constructive moves include his treatment of *kevalānvayi* inferences, his analysis of *pratyakṣa* in terms compatible with *sākṣi-pramāṇa*, and his refusal to grant the Naiyāyika understanding of *abhāva* as an autonomous *padārtha* — *abhāva* on his account collapses into a mode of the *adhikaraṇa*, sparing the school the regress-problems it would otherwise inherit. The *Tarka-Tāṇḍava* is the natural companion of the *Nyāyāmṛta*: where the *Nyāyāmṛta* fights the Advaitins, the *Tāṇḍava* fights the Naiyāyikas, and together they make Vyāsatīrtha's *Navya-Dvaita* a genuine third party in the late-medieval dialectical landscape. Rāghavendra's *Pariṃala* is its principal sub-commentary.

engaged_works.title: Tatparya-Chandrika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Jayatīrtha's Tattva-Prakāśikā.
engaged_works.summary: Vyāsatīrtha's sub-sub-commentary on Jayatīrtha's *Tattva-Prakāśikā* — and so a third-order text on Madhva's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. The *Tātparya-Candrikā* ("Moon of Purport") moves through the *Sūtras* *adhikaraṇa* by *adhikaraṇa*, taking each of Jayatīrtha's glosses and (a) defending it against opposed readings developed in the intervening generations (especially Appayya-line and Vivaraṇa critiques), (b) extending it into Navya-Nyāya form where Jayatīrtha had argued in older *prācīna* style, and (c) drawing connections to the *Anuvyākhyāna*–*Nyāya-Sudhā* sequence so that the school's commentarial layers can be read as a single argued position. The *Candrikā* is the final piece of the canonical Mādhva commentarial stack on the *Sūtra* (*Sūtra* → Madhva's *Bhāṣya* → Jayatīrtha's *Tattva-Prakāśikā* → Vyāsatīrtha's *Tātparya-Candrikā*), on which Rāghavendra builds the *Candrikā-Prakāśa* and other late *prakāśa*-glosses. Less polemical than the *Nyāyāmṛta* and *Tāṇḍava*, it is the indispensable text for the school's reading of the *Brahma-Sūtra* itself.

pada_analysis.gloss: whose infinite auspicious qualities are declared

key_passages.english_close: I worship the consort of Śrī, whose infinite auspicious qualities are declared, with reverent regard, by self-valid Vedas devoid of an originating author.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This opening verse is not merely devotional ornament: it condenses Vyāsatīrtha's epistemic and theological commitments in one place, making Veda self-valid and authorless while presenting the Lord as the bearer of infinite auspicious qualities. Given that the accessible on-disk corpus for this run is commentary-inclusive and not a clean critical base text throughout, the work's own invocatory verse is one of the safest representative loci to anchor the selection.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext (`sa_vyAsatIrtha-tarkatANDava-comm.txt`), converted 2020-07-31 from the Sansknet Project source; includes Rāghavendra Tīrtha's Nyāyadīpa commentary.

key_passages.locus_long: Tarka-Tāṇḍava, pariccheda 1, prāmāṇyavāda, opening Vedāntin–Naiyāyika dispute-triad
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
prāmāṇyaṃ svata iti vedāntinaḥ /
parata iti naiyāyikāḥ //
vedāḥ apauruṣeyā nityāśceti vedāntinaḥ /
īśvaroccaritā anityā iti naiyāyikāḥ //
īśvarasya sārvajñyādikaṃ vedenaiva siddhyatīti vedāntinaḥ /
dharmāgrāhakānumānair iti naiyāyikāḥ //

karaka_structure.note: elliptically supplies the Naiyāyika side of the same predication

key_passages.english_close: Vedāntins say: validity is intrinsic; Naiyāyikas: it is extrinsic. Vedāntins say: the Vedas are authorless and eternal; Naiyāyikas: they are uttered by God and non-eternal. Vedāntins say: the Lord's omniscience and the rest are established by the Veda alone; Naiyāyikas: by inferences that apprehend dharma.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This opening dispute-triad is the clearest available map of the work's argumentative front: intrinsic validity, Vedic authorlessness, and the Veda-grounded proof of a fully qualified Lord. Because the on-disk source is commentary-inclusive, I use this locus explicitly as the file's own framing of Vyāsatīrtha's first pariccheda rather than pretending it is an isolated aphorism torn from context.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext (`sa_vyAsatIrtha-tarkatANDava-comm.txt`), converted 2020-07-31 from the Sansknet Project source; includes Rāghavendra Tīrtha's Nyāyadīpa commentary.

key_passages.locus_long: Tarka-Tāṇḍava, pariccheda 1, prāmāṇyavāda, lakṣaṇa of svatastva in origination

pada_analysis.gloss: origination dependent solely on the producer of the cognition

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: jñānajanakasya mātrasya adhīnaṃ janma yasya tat

key_passages.english_close: Svatastva is origination dependent solely on the producer of the cognition.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: intrinsicness, non-derivation from a further external factor

key_passages.why_this_passage: This compressed lakṣaṇa is worth selecting because it states Vyāsatīrtha's positive thesis without dialectical scaffolding: for him, intrinsic validity at the level of origination means that no extra validity-producing factor beyond the cognition's own producer is required. It is not a famous ornamental line but a load-bearing definition inside the prāmāṇyavāda.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext (`sa_vyAsatIrtha-tarkatANDava-comm.txt`), converted 2020-07-31 from the Sansknet Project source; includes Rāghavendra Tīrtha's Nyāyadīpa commentary.

key_passages.locus_long: Tarka-Tāṇḍava, pariccheda 1, prāmāṇyavāda, inference for the intrinsic graspability of cognitive validity
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
jñānaprāmāṇyaṃ svato grāhyaṃ,
parato 'gṛhyamāṇatve sati gṛhyamāṇatvāt /
yadyad anyenāgṛhyamāṇatve sati grāhyaṃ
tat tena grāhyaṃ,
yathā cakṣur anyenāgṛhyamāṇaṃ rūpaṃ
cakṣurgrāhyam //

pada_analysis.gloss: in the state of not being grasped
in the state of not being grasped

karaka_structure.note: predicate subject of the inferential statement
instrument in the universal concomitance clause
object of the illustrative eye-grasp case
understood instrument in the example; compacted in cakṣurgrāhyam

key_passages.english_close: The validity of a cognition is intrinsically graspable, because, while not being grasped by something else, it is nevertheless grasped. Whatever, while not being grasped by another, is grasped, that is grasped by that very thing itself; just as a visible form, not grasped by another, is grasped by the eye.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the work's most explicit compact inferential defense of svato-grāhyatva, and so it shows Vyāsatīrtha doing what makes him distinctive: casting a Dvaita epistemic thesis in sharp Navya-style inferential form. It fairly represents the text because the surrounding section is devoted precisely to defending intrinsic validity against Naiyāyika externalism.
key_passages.source_edition: GRETIL plaintext (`sa_vyAsatIrtha-tarkatANDava-comm.txt`), converted 2020-07-31 from the Sansknet Project source; includes Rāghavendra Tīrtha's Nyāyadīpa commentary.
